title: Quantum Networks Using Color Defects in Diamond: Principles, Progress, and Perspectives
authors: Ayan Majumder, Cem Güney Torun, Tim Schröder, Gregor Pieplow, Prem Kumar, Kasturi Saha
abstract: Large-scale quantum networks will enable entirely new applications of quantum information science in fields such as quantum communication, distributed quantum computing, sensing, and metrology. To build nodes of such networks, diamond color defects are one of the promising candidates. Their excellent optical properties, fast spin-qubit control, and long spin coherence times make them well-suited for quantum information processing and quantum memory applications. Additionally, recent advances in the heterogeneous integration of diamond nanophotonic structures with photonic integrated circuits have made these systems more efficient and well-suited for scalable quantum processor architectures. In this comprehensive review, we discuss the optical and spin properties of these systems, recent progress in the building blocks of quantum networks, and demonstrations of metropolitan-scale quantum networks, as well as the challenges associated with these systems at both the fundamental and experimental levels, along with potential solutions.
summary: This review surveys the use of diamond color defects (like NV and SiV centers) as robust solid-state nodes for building large-scale quantum networks. It details the necessary quantum control techniques, compares the physical merits of different defects, and emphasizes the critical challenge of integrating these emitters with scalable photonic circuits for practical quantum computation and communication.
main_problem: The primary goal is realizing large-scale quantum networks necessary for advanced quantum information applications like distributed computing and quantum communication.
main_result: Diamond color defects, such as NV and group-IV vacancies, are highly promising solid-state nodes due to their excellent optical properties, long coherence times, and potential for integration with photonic circuits.
method: The review discusses theoretical modeling of spin-photon interactions, quantum repeater protocols (REPs), and experimental progress in hybrid integration with advanced photonic platforms.
model_or_system: The core system is the solid-state quantum node based on color defects in diamond (NV, SiV, etc.). These nodes utilize electronic spins as communication qubits and nuclear spins as long-lived memory qubits, interacting via optical and microwave control.
key_observables: Coherence times ($T_2$, $T_2^n$), entanglement generation rates, quantum gate fidelity, and coupling efficiencies to integrated photonics.
important_parameters: Operating temperature, Zero-Phonon-Line (ZPL) wavelength, Debye-Waller factor, and the energy splitting ($\Delta_{GS}$) of the defect centers.
main_assumptions: The review assumes the feasibility of hybrid integration between diamond emitters and mature photonic platforms like SiN or LiNbO3 to achieve scalability.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate network schematics, the three main remote-entanglement protocol architectures, and energy level diagrams detailing spin and optical transitions for various defects.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from defining the need for quantum networks, detailing the physical properties and comparison of different color defects (NV vs. group-IV), discussing the underlying quantum control mechanisms, and concluding with the challenges and solutions for scalable hybrid integration.
why_interesting: It provides a comprehensive overview of solid-state quantum memories and processing units, which is crucial for understanding the physical implementation layer of quantum information science, bridging quantum optics and solid-state physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of a large-scale quantum network composed of solid-state nodes (dark blue) that host optically interfaced communication and memory qubits. Photons are distributed through optical fibers or free-space channels to generate entan- glement between distant nodes. Quantum repeaters, such as those based on midpoint-measurement–based systems, indicated by yellow circles and illustrated on the right, enhance entanglement generation rates over long distances by mitigating photon transmission losses. The bottom inset depicts a hybrid integrated quantum device functioning as a quantum node, where an optically active spin defect center with a nuclear-spin register embedded in diamond...

FIG. 2. (a) The topology for remote entanglement generation is decomposed into its fundamental logical building blocks. From left to right, the three main remote-entanglement protocol (REP) architectures are illustrated: the detection-in-midpoint, sender–receiver, and source-in-midpoint topologies. This illustration is adapted from Ref. [44]. (b) The potential energies of the ground and excited electronic states of an optically active solid-state quantum system are shown as functions of the collective nuclear displacement coordinate. This illustration is adapted from Ref. [15]. (c) Radiative and non-radiative emission of a solid-state quantum emitter in an optical collection system, i.e.,...

FIG. 3. (a) Atomic configuration of the NV defect inside a diamond lattice unit cell. (b) Molecular orbitals (MO) and corresponding electronic distribution in the ground state of the NV center [62]. (c) Electronic energy-level diagram of the negatively charged nitrogen vacancy defect center in a diamond crystal. This illustration is adapted from Refs. [12, 56].

FIG. 4. (a) Atomic configuration of a negatively charged group-IV defect within a diamond lattice unit cell. (b) The orbital coherence time T orbital 1 depends on the ground state splitting and temperature. The colormap gives the simulated values using χρ = 4.6 × 10−9 GHz−2. The circles indicate measured values of SiV, GeV, and SnV centers. The T spin 1 and T ∗,spin 2 values have been converted to the corresponding T orbital 1 . The experimental values can be lower than the simulations as the coherence can be limited by other sources. This illustration is adapted from Ref. [56]. FIG. 5. (a) Excited-state transitions of the NV center were resolved using a tunable red laser scanned across the resonance. This illustration is adapted from Ref. [119]. The inset figure shows the emission spectrum of the NV centers at 4 K under 532 nm laser excitation. (b) Low-temperature emission spectra of group-IV color centers in diamond. This figure is adapted from Ref. [120].
